concept:material_breach

utterance.text: A material breach under contract law occurs when a party fails to perform a substantial obligation that goes to the essence of the agreement, depriving the non-breaching party of the benefit it reasonably expected—applied to SLA clause 14.2, this means a failure to maintain the guaranteed uptime threshold (typically 99.9%) constitutes a material breach because continuous service availability is a fundamental obligation upon which the customer's reliance and the contract's core value depend. The non-breaching party would, upon such failure and any applicable cure-period expiration, be entitled to invoke termination rights and pursue remedies (including service credits, damages, or both) as specified under the agreement.

context.topic: concept:material_breach

utterance.text: I believe that under the material breach analysis, a party who has completed 85% of their deliverables by the Day 60 milestone has achieved substantial performance and therefore cannot be found in material breach, since the remaining 15% constitutes only minor deviations that are compensable through offset rather than triggering termination rights or consequential damages exposure. This timeline-based completion threshold, in my view, converts what might otherwise be a breach inquiry into a mere damages quantification exercise, precluding the non-breaching party from invoking indemnity or SLA penalty clauses.

context.topic: concept:substantial_performance
concept:material_breach

utterance.text: I need to flag a critical issue. We have identified a material breach condition that requires an immediate hard stop on current proceedings. All activities under this agreement must cease until the breach is formally addressed and remediated.
